stem: A 7-year-old boy is referred to the paediatric emergency department with two weeks of polyuria, polydipsia, and 4 kg weight loss. Today he developed vomiting, abdominal pain, and laboured breathing. He weighs 22 kg. He is alert, oriented, with deep sighing respirations and acetone breath. He has not previously been diagnosed with diabetes and has no shock features clinically, though he is moderately dehydrated.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132, RR 36, SpO2 98% room air, BP 98/62, Temp 36.6
data_table.Examination: Kussmaul breathing; dry mucous membranes; CRT 2s; alert (GCS 15); no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: BSL 28.4 mmol/L, ketones 5.8 mmol/L; venous pH 7.10, bicarbonate 8 mmol/L, sodium 134 mmol/L, potassium 4.6 mmol/L, urea 9.2 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial fluid management?

options.text: Intravenous 0.9 percent sodium chloride 20 mL/kg bolus over 15 minutes then reassess for further boluses
options.rationale: Aggressive 20 mL/kg boluses in non-shocked paediatric DKA increase cerebral oedema risk; bolus volumes are reserved for circulatory shock and given as 10 mL/kg with reassessment. (Source: APEG/RCH Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous 0.9 percent sodium chloride 10 mL/kg over 30 minutes then maintenance plus deficit replacement over 48 hours
options.rationale: Australian paediatric DKA protocols use a single 10 mL/kg bolus only when perfusion is impaired, then estimate dehydration at 7 to 8 percent and replace the deficit evenly over 48 hours alongside maintenance to minimise cerebral oedema. (Source: APEG/RCH Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous Hartmann solution 30 mL/kg over an hour with intravenous insulin started immediately at induction
options.rationale: Large volume Hartmann boluses risk dilutional fall in osmolality and rapid bicarbonate correction with cerebral oedema; insulin started before intravascular volume restoration accelerates the osmotic shift. (Source: APEG/RCH Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Oral rehydration solution titrated to thirst with bedside ketone reassessment every two hours before any insulin
options.rationale: Oral rehydration is inadequate in vomiting children with significant acidosis; intravenous access and controlled deficit replacement with insulin infusion are mandatory. (Source: APEG/RCH Paediatric DKA Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous sodium bicarbonate 1 mmol/kg over 30 minutes given the venous pH below 7.15 with normal saline alongside
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is not indicated in paediatric DKA except in life-threatening hyperkalaemia or cardiac arrest; routine use worsens intracellular acidosis and increases cerebral oedema risk. (Source: APEG/RCH Paediatric DKA Guideline)

explanation.summary: Paediatric DKA fluid management balances volume restoration against cerebral oedema risk. A single 10 mL/kg 0.9 percent sodium chloride bolus is given only for circulatory compromise. Dehydration is estimated at 7 to 8 percent and replaced evenly over 48 hours alongside maintenance, with insulin started 1 hour after fluids at 0.05 to 0.1 units/kg/h. Bicarbonate is contraindicated outside arrest.
key_points: Bolus only for shock; single 10 mL/kg dose then reassess
Deficit replaced over 48 hours, not 24
Insulin starts 1 hour after fluids at 0.05 to 0.1 units/kg/h
Bicarbonate worsens outcomes in paediatric DKA
explanation.pearls: Australian APEG paediatric DKA protocol: 10 mL/kg saline bolus only if shocked, then maintenance plus 7 to 8 percent deficit over 48 hours, insulin at 0.05 to 0.1 units/kg/h starting 1 hour after fluids.

sources.label: ISPAD Clinical Practice Consensus Guidelines: DKA

stem: A 2-year-old girl presents to a metropolitan emergency department at 02:00 with a 12-hour history of coryza and a barking cough. Over the last hour she has developed audible inspiratory stridor at rest and is visibly distressed. She weighs 14 kg and has no past medical history. Her vaccinations are up to date. She has not yet received any treatment in the department.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 148, RR 38, SpO2 95% room air, Temp 37.8
data_table.Examination: Inspiratory stridor at rest; suprasternal and intercostal recession; tracheal tug; agitated but rousable
data_table.Investigations: Clinical diagnosis; no imaging or bloods required

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate treatment?

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single dose with reassessment of stridor severity after 30 minutes
options.rationale: Oral dexamethasone is correct for mild to moderate croup, but severe croup with stridor at rest plus retractions warrants concurrent nebulised adrenaline for rapid relief. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 1:1000 5 mL with concurrent oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg and minimum 4 hour observation
options.rationale: Stridor at rest with retractions defines severe croup; nebulised adrenaline gives rapid relief lasting 30 to 60 minutes and a single corticosteroid dose extends effect, with mandatory observation post-adrenaline for rebound. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg back to back with ipratropium 250 micrograms then admission to short-stay unit
options.rationale: Bronchodilators target lower airway bronchospasm and have no benefit in viral laryngotracheitis where the obstruction is subglottic oedema. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg plus immediate ENT review with airway plan for theatre intubation
options.rationale: Empiric ceftriaxone with ENT is the pathway for suspected epiglottitis where the child is toxic, drooling, and sitting in tripod position; the barking cough and coryza here are typical viral croup. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

options.text: Humidified oxygen via head box with a 30 minute trial of mist therapy before any pharmacological treatment
options.rationale: Cochrane reviews show humidified air provides no benefit in croup and delays effective treatment; severe croup mandates adrenaline and steroid without delay. (Source: RCH CPG: Croup)

explanation.summary: Severe croup is defined by stridor at rest plus retractions or agitation. Nebulised adrenaline 1:1000 5 mL gives rapid relief within 10 minutes lasting around 60 minutes, and oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg provides sustained corticosteroid effect. Children must be observed for 3 hours (RCH minimum) after-adrenaline because of potential rebound stridor before discharge can be considered.
key_points: Stridor at rest plus retractions = severe croup
Adrenaline acts in 10 minutes; effect wanes by 60 minutes
Dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg PO for severe disease
Observe minimum 4 hours post-adrenaline for rebound
explanation.pearls: Severe croup: nebulised 1:1000 adrenaline 5 mL plus oral dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg, observe 4 hours minimum because rebound stridor follows the 60-minute adrenaline window.

stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a 3-day palpable purpuric rash over the buttocks and lower limbs, intermittent crampy abdominal pain, and an acutely swollen painful left knee. He had a coryzal illness two weeks ago. Today his urinalysis shows 1+ blood and no protein. His blood pressure is normal and his renal function is preserved. The family lives 90 minutes from the nearest paediatric centre and asks whether they need to remain admitted. The diagnosis has been discussed with the family.

data_table.Examination: Palpable non-blanching purpura on lower limbs and buttocks; tender left knee with effusion; soft non-tender abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Urine dipstick blood 1+, protein nil; Cr 38 micromol/L; FBC normal; platelets 312 x10^9/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate follow-up plan for renal involvement?

options.text: Urine dipstick and blood pressure measurement weekly for one month then monthly to 6 months and at 12 months
options.rationale: Nephritis can present up to 6 months after initial rash even when urinalysis is initially normal; the recommended surveillance is weekly to 4 weeks then monthly to 6 months with a final review at 12 months. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Renal ultrasound now followed by routine paediatrician review in 6 weeks with paired urea and electrolytes
options.rationale: Renal ultrasound is not part of routine HSP surveillance and does not detect early nephritis; urinalysis and blood pressure are the screening tools that pick up significant proteinuria first. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Discharge with general practitioner urinalysis once at 4 weeks unless rash, joint, or abdominal symptoms recur
options.rationale: A single 4-week urinalysis misses the delayed nephritis window; up to 30 percent develop renal involvement within 6 months and weekly then monthly surveillance is needed. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Empirical oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg for 2 weeks plus weekly urinalysis until rash resolves then discharge
options.rationale: Routine corticosteroids do not prevent nephritis or reduce long-term renal sequelae and are not justified solely to influence rash duration. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

options.text: Urgent paediatric nephrology referral and percutaneous renal biopsy within 2 weeks regardless of progression
options.rationale: Biopsy is reserved for nephrotic-range proteinuria, persistent significant proteinuria over 4 weeks, or rapidly deteriorating function; isolated microscopic haematuria does not warrant invasive investigation. (Source: RCH CPG: Henoch-Schonlein purpura)

explanation.summary: Nephritis in HSP can present up to 6 months after the initial rash, and around one third of children develop urinary abnormalities. The Australian surveillance schedule includes weekly urinalysis and blood pressure for the first month, then monthly to 6 months, with a final review at 12 months. Persistent proteinuria, hypertension, or declining function trigger nephrology referral and potential biopsy.
key_points: Nephritis may appear up to 6 months after rash
Surveillance: weekly to 4 weeks, monthly to 6 months, then 12 months
Steroids do not prevent or treat nephritis prophylactically
Biopsy thresholds: nephrotic proteinuria, persistent significant proteinuria, or declining GFR
explanation.pearls: Even with normal initial urinalysis, HSP requires weekly BP and dipstick to 4 weeks then monthly to 6 months and review at 12 months because nephritis can present late.

sources.label: KDIGO Clinical Practice Guideline for Glomerular Diseases

subtopic_detail: Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy escalation
stem: A 29-year-old G3P2 at 28 weeks gestation is reviewed at antenatal clinic. She has fatigue and breathlessness on stairs. She has been taking oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily for 6 weeks since her booking bloods showed mild iron deficiency anaemia, but reports persistent nausea and constipation and admits she has missed doses. Her current bloods reveal further drop in haemoglobin and ferritin. She has no history of bleeding or malabsorption and is up to date with antenatal care.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, BP 110/68, SpO2 98% room air, Temp 36.6
data_table.Examination: Mild conjunctival pallor; symphysis-fundal height appropriate; foetal heart 142
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L, MCV 76 fL, ferritin 12 micrograms/L, CRP 3 mg/L; B12 and folate normal

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Switch to oral ferrous fumarate 200 mg three times daily and review the haemoglobin in 4 weeks for response
options.rationale: Increasing oral iron dose worsens gastrointestinal side effects, reduces adherence further, and rarely closes a haemoglobin gap before delivery; intravenous iron is recommended when oral therapy has failed. (Source: RANZCOG: Iron Deficiency in Pregnancy)

options.text: Arrange intravenous ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg as a single outpatient infusion and continue antenatal care
options.rationale: After demonstrated oral iron failure or intolerance in the second or third trimester, intravenous iron is the recommended treatment to restore stores before delivery; ferric carboxymaltose can be given in one outpatient infusion. (Source: RANZCOG: Iron Deficiency in Pregnancy)

options.text: Transfuse 2 units of red cells now given haemoglobin under 95 g/L and approaching delivery within 12 weeks
options.rationale: Transfusion is reserved for symptomatic anaemia with haemodynamic compromise or planned delivery with risk of significant haemorrhage; routine antenatal transfusion at 92 g/L is not indicated. (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management Module 5: Obstetrics)
source_refs: NBA Patient Blood Management Module 5: Obstetrics

options.text: Request bone marrow biopsy through haematology to exclude underlying haemoglobinopathy and aplastic process
options.rationale: Bone marrow biopsy is invasive and not indicated when ferritin under 30 micrograms/L with microcytic indices already establishes iron deficiency; haemoglobinopathy screen would be a less invasive next step if relevant. (Source: RANZCOG: Iron Deficiency in Pregnancy)

options.text: Recommend cessation of all iron supplementation and dietary intervention with red meat and vitamin C combinations
options.rationale: Dietary iron alone cannot correct established iron deficiency anaemia with ferritin 12 in the third trimester; abandoning supplementation guarantees worsening anaemia before birth. (Source: RANZCOG: Iron Deficiency in Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency anaemia in pregnancy with ferritin below 30 micrograms/L is treated with oral iron first line. Failure of response, intolerance, or presentation in late pregnancy with insufficient time to correct stores triggers intravenous iron. Ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg as a single infusion is the most common Australian regimen and can be given from the second trimester onward. Transfusion is reserved for haemodynamic compromise or imminent delivery with anticipated haemorrhage.
key_points: Oral iron first line if tolerated and time to correct
IV iron from second trimester if oral failure or intolerance
Ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg single infusion is the AU standard
Transfusion only for haemodynamic compromise or imminent haemorrhage risk
explanation.pearls: RANZCOG and NBA: IV ferric carboxymaltose 1000 mg single infusion from 2nd trimester onwards when oral iron fails or is poorly tolerated, with ferritin under 30 micrograms/L.

sources.label: NBA Patient Blood Management Module 5: Obstetrics

stem: A 33-year-old G2P1 attends for her routine gestational diabetes screen at 26 weeks gestation. Her previous baby was 4.3 kg at 39 weeks with shoulder dystocia. Her BMI is 31. She has no family history of diabetes but was unable to attend earlier screening due to severe nausea. She underwent a fasting 75 gram oral glucose tolerance test today after an overnight fast. She is asymptomatic and has otherwise had an uncomplicated antenatal course.

data_table.Examination: Symphysis-fundal height 27 cm; foetal heart 148; appropriate growth on USS at 22 weeks
data_table.Investigations: 75g OGTT: fasting 5.3 mmol/L, 1 hour 9.8 mmol/L, 2 hour 7.6 mmol/L

lead_in: What does this OGTT result indicate?

options.text: Normal OGTT with no further glucose testing required and routine antenatal care continued to term
options.rationale: ADIPS uses fasting at least 5.1, 1 hour at least 10.0, and 2 hour at least 8.5 mmol/L; the 1 hour 9.8 here is below the 1 hour cut-off but the 2 hour value warrants attention if any threshold met. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines 2014)

options.text: Gestational diabetes confirmed because the fasting and 2 hour values both meet ADIPS diagnostic thresholds
options.rationale: The fasting value is 5.3 which exceeds the ADIPS fasting cut-off of 5.1, but the 2 hour value of 7.6 is below the 2 hour cut-off of 8.5; only the fasting criterion is met. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines 2014)

options.text: Gestational diabetes diagnosed on the basis of the elevated fasting glucose result alone meeting threshold
options.rationale: ADIPS diagnoses GDM if any single value is reached: fasting at least 5.1, 1 hour at least 10.0, or 2 hour at least 8.5 mmol/L; this patient meets the fasting cut-off so diagnosis is confirmed. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines 2014)

options.text: Impaired glucose tolerance only with planned repeat 75g OGTT performed at 34 weeks gestation for confirmation
options.rationale: Pregnancy uses GDM diagnostic criteria that differ from non-pregnant IGT thresholds; meeting any ADIPS value confirms GDM and there is no diagnostic repeat in pregnancy. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines 2014)

options.text: Overt diabetes in pregnancy requiring immediate referral for insulin therapy and high-risk diabetes pregnancy clinic
options.rationale: Overt diabetes thresholds are fasting at least 7.0 or HbA1c at least 6.5 percent or random at least 11.1 mmol/L; this patient meets GDM but not overt diabetes criteria. (Source: ADIPS Consensus Guidelines 2014)

explanation.summary: ADIPS diagnoses gestational diabetes when at least one 75 gram OGTT value meets: fasting at least 5.1 mmol/L, 1 hour at least 10.0 mmol/L, or 2 hour at least 8.5 mmol/L. Diagnosis triggers diabetes-in-pregnancy clinic referral, dietary review, glucose monitoring, and consideration of metformin or insulin if targets not met within 1 to 2 weeks. Overt diabetes is a separate diagnosis with higher thresholds.
key_points: ADIPS fasting cut-off 5.1 mmol/L
ADIPS 1-hour cut-off 10.0 mmol/L
ADIPS 2-hour cut-off 8.5 mmol/L
Any single value meeting threshold confirms GDM
explanation.pearls: Australian ADIPS 75g OGTT thresholds for GDM: fasting at least 5.1, 1 hour at least 10.0, 2 hour at least 8.5 mmol/L. Any one value reached makes the diagnosis.

subtopic_detail: Severe pre-eclampsia magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis
stem: A 27-year-old G1 at 35+4 weeks gestation presents to the birth suite with severe right upper quadrant pain and a headache that began this morning. Her blood pressure on arrival is 168/112 despite oral labetalol 200 mg given two hours ago. She has new visual scintillations and brisk reflexes with two beats of clonus. Her urinalysis shows protein 3+ and her bloods reveal an LDH and transaminase pattern consistent with disease progression. The neonatal team is alerted and the on-call obstetric consultant is notified.

data_table.Examination: Tender right upper quadrant; hyperreflexia with 2 beats clonus; visual scintillations; foetal heart 140 reassuring
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 92 x10^9/L, ALT 142 U/L, AST 138 U/L, LDH 480 U/L, Cr 78 micromol/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L, urine PCR 320 mg/mmol

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g loading dose over 20 minutes followed by 1 g per hour maintenance infusion
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia with headache, visual symptoms, hyperreflexia, clonus, and biochemical progression requires magnesium sulfate for eclampsia prophylaxis at 4 g IV over 20 minutes then 1 g/h, alongside antihypertensive escalation. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous diazepam 10 mg slow bolus with intramuscular promethazine 25 mg for sedation and seizure prophylaxis
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are second-line for established eclamptic seizures only after magnesium has failed; magnesium sulfate has the lowest maternal mortality and neonatal sedation profile for prophylaxis. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous phenytoin 18 mg/kg loading infusion over 30 minutes followed by 100 mg every 8 hours by mouth
options.rationale: Phenytoin is less effective than magnesium sulfate at preventing eclamptic seizures and carries proarrhythmic risk during loading; the MAGPIE trial and SOMANZ favour magnesium. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous dexamethasone 12 mg with delivery delayed for a full 24 hour interval to allow neonatal lung maturity
options.rationale: Corticosteroids beyond 34 weeks are not routinely indicated and delivery in severe pre-eclampsia with end-organ progression should not be delayed to complete a corticosteroid course at 35 weeks. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

options.text: Intravenous mannitol 0.25 g/kg over 30 minutes to reduce cerebral oedema and prevent neurological deterioration
options.rationale: Mannitol has no role in pre-eclampsia and adds fluid loading risk in a syndrome characterised by reduced intravascular volume; magnesium sulfate is the seizure prophylaxis of choice. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy Guideline)

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia with cerebral or hepatic features mandates magnesium sulfate for eclampsia prophylaxis with the Zuspan regimen of 4 g IV over 20 minutes then 1 g per hour, alongside antihypertensive escalation to keep systolic under 160 and diastolic under 110. Delivery follows once maternal stabilisation is achieved. Mode of delivery balances maternal and foetal status; at 35 weeks with end-organ disease, delivery is indicated.
key_points: Magnesium sulfate 4 g loading then 1 g/h is the Australian seizure prophylaxis
Antihypertensive target BP under 160/110
Magnesium is superior to phenytoin and diazepam (MAGPIE trial)
Delivery is the definitive treatment once stabilised
explanation.pearls: SOMANZ severe pre-eclampsia: magnesium sulfate 4 g IV loading over 20 minutes then 1 g/h infusion; this gives lowest maternal mortality and neonatal sedation per the MAGPIE trial.

subtopic_detail: Adult anaphylaxis immediate IM adrenaline dose
stem: A 38-year-old man presents to the emergency department after 15 minutes of progressive lip and tongue swelling, audible wheeze, and lightheadedness during a restaurant meal. He has a known allergy to tree nuts and forgot to bring his autoinjector. On arrival he is anxious, generalised urticaria is visible, his tongue is swollen but he is still phonating, and his peripheries are cool. He weighs 78 kg. He has no other medical conditions and is on no regular medications.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 128, RR 28, SpO2 92% room air, BP 86/52, Temp 36.5
data_table.Examination: Generalised urticaria; tongue swelling without stridor; bilateral wheeze; CRT 4 seconds; alert
data_table.Investigations: Not performed; allergy referral after stabilisation

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Intramuscular adrenaline 0.5 mg of 1:1000 solution into the anterolateral mid-thigh and prompt reassessment cycle
options.rationale: ASCIA recommends 0.5 mg IM adrenaline from a 1:1000 ampoule into the anterolateral mid-thigh for adults with anaphylaxis featuring airway or circulatory involvement, repeated every 5 minutes if response is inadequate. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Intravenous adrenaline 1 mg of 1:10000 solution given as a slow push under continuous cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Intravenous adrenaline in anaphylaxis is reserved for refractory cases or arrest under intensive monitoring; first-line in spontaneously breathing adults is intramuscular dosing because of the lower arrhythmia risk. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg with intravenous promethazine 25 mg as initial pharmacological treatment given
options.rationale: Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts and do not treat the airway and cardiovascular components of anaphylaxis; delaying adrenaline to give them increases mortality. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mg with high-flow oxygen 15 L per minute and a 1 litre crystalloid bolus through wide cannula
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline targets upper airway oedema as adjunct only; systemic anaphylaxis with hypotension requires intramuscular adrenaline as the immediate primary intervention. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

options.text: Subcutaneous adrenaline 0.5 mg from a 1:1000 ampoule given into the upper arm with airway adjunct preparation
options.rationale: Subcutaneous adrenaline has slow and unpredictable absorption due to peripheral vasoconstriction; intramuscular delivery into the thigh is the correct route. (Source: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis)

explanation.summary: Adult anaphylaxis is treated with 0.5 mg intramuscular adrenaline from a 1:1000 ampoule injected into the anterolateral mid-thigh. The dose is repeated every 5 minutes if response is inadequate, with concurrent supine positioning, high-flow oxygen, intravenous crystalloid for hypotension, and salbutamol or nebulised adrenaline as adjuncts. Intravenous adrenaline is reserved for refractory cases under monitoring.
key_points: Adult IM adrenaline dose is 0.5 mg of 1:1000
Anterolateral thigh, not deltoid or subcutaneous
Repeat every 5 minutes if no improvement
IV adrenaline only for refractory anaphylaxis under monitoring
explanation.pearls: ASCIA adult anaphylaxis: 0.5 mg of 1:1000 adrenaline IM into anterolateral mid-thigh, repeated every 5 minutes if needed; IV reserved for refractory cases.

stem: A 56-year-old man with a bioprosthetic aortic valve replaced 14 months ago presents with three weeks of fever, weight loss, and night sweats. He has had two intervening dental procedures without prophylaxis. His blood cultures from two separate venepuncture sites taken 12 hours apart have grown viridans group streptococci. A transthoracic echocardiogram is poor quality and inconclusive. A transoesophageal echo is awaited. The patient is otherwise haemodynamically stable on the ward.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102, BP 118/72, RR 20, SpO2 96% room air, Temp 38.4
data_table.Examination: Soft early diastolic murmur at left sternal edge; nil splinter haemorrhages or Janeway lesions noted today
data_table.Investigations: WCC 14.2 x10^9/L, CRP 102 mg/L, Hb 105 g/L, Cr 92 micromol/L; ECG sinus tachycardia, no new conduction; TTE non-diagnostic

lead_in: How is this patient classified under the modified Duke criteria at present?

options.text: Definite infective endocarditis on the strength of pathological criteria from histology and microbiology already
options.rationale: Pathological Duke criteria require tissue or vegetation specimens with histological or microbiological proof; without surgery these are not available, and definite IE must be reached via clinical criteria. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guideline 2023)

options.text: Possible infective endocarditis pending transoesophageal echo result and a documented new regurgitant murmur
options.rationale: Possible IE applies when one major and one minor, or three minor criteria, are met; this patient already has two major criteria once typical organism in two cultures is combined with the new regurgitant murmur evidence. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guideline 2023)

options.text: Definite infective endocarditis fulfilled by two clinical major criteria from typical organism and new regurgitation
options.rationale: Two separate cultures of viridans streptococci constitute one major criterion, and a new regurgitant murmur in a prosthetic valve patient constitutes the second major (evidence of endocardial involvement); two major criteria satisfy definite IE. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guideline 2023)

options.text: Rejected diagnosis because transthoracic echocardiogram failed to visualise vegetations and patient remains stable
options.rationale: A non-diagnostic TTE does not reject IE; in prosthetic valves TOE is required and patients with persistent bacteraemia and typical organisms remain in possible or definite categories. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guideline 2023)

options.text: Definite infective endocarditis based on one major criterion only with multiple supportive minor criteria currently
options.rationale: One major plus three minor would meet definite IE, or two major; the question stem documents one major (organism) and the new murmur (a second major), so the categorisation rests on two major criteria rather than the minor mix. (Source: ESC Endocarditis Guideline 2023)

explanation.summary: Modified Duke criteria diagnose definite IE when two major, or one major and three minor, or five minor clinical criteria are met. Major criteria include typical organisms in two blood cultures from separate sites and evidence of endocardial involvement (vegetation, abscess, new partial dehiscence, or new regurgitant murmur). Pathological diagnosis requires tissue from surgery or autopsy. A negative TTE does not reject IE; TOE is required in prosthetic valves and high-risk cases.
key_points: Definite IE: 2 major, or 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor
Typical organism in 2 separate cultures = major
New regurgitant murmur or vegetation = major
Negative TTE never rejects IE in prosthetic valves; TOE mandatory
explanation.pearls: Two separate cultures of viridans strep plus a new regurgitant murmur in a prosthetic valve patient = two Duke major criteria = definite IE without needing echo confirmation.

subtopic_detail: Schizoaffective disorder vs bipolar with psychosis
stem: A 28-year-old woman is admitted for the third time in two years with persecutory delusions, auditory hallucinations of two voices arguing, and disorganised thought. Records show two distinct presentations: an 8-week episode of severe depression with congruent psychotic features 18 months ago, and a 3-week manic presentation with grandiose delusions 10 months ago. Between episodes she has continued to report ongoing auditory hallucinations and persecutory beliefs persisting for at least 4 weeks at a stretch without prominent mood symptoms. Substance use and medical causes are excluded.

data_table.Examination: Disorganised speech; second-person auditory hallucinations; persecutory delusions; mood currently mildly elevated
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen negative; TSH normal; CT brain unremarkable; B12 normal

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder, bipolar type, given mood episodes alongside persistent psychotic symptoms without mood
options.rationale: DSM-5 schizoaffective disorder requires a major mood episode concurrent with criterion A schizophrenia symptoms plus at least 2 weeks of psychotic symptoms without prominent mood; presence of both manic and depressive episodes qualifies as the bipolar subtype. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with psychotic features only present during episodes of mania or major depressive disturbance
options.rationale: Bipolar I with psychotic features confines psychosis to mood episodes; documented psychosis lasting at least 2 weeks without prominent mood symptoms rules this out. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophrenia with secondary mood symptoms occurring as a brief minor reaction to ongoing psychotic experience
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires that any mood episodes be brief relative to the total duration of psychosis; here two distinct full mood episodes meeting duration criteria exclude that pattern. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Major depressive disorder with mood-congruent psychotic features and incidental persistent perceptual phenomena later
options.rationale: MDD with psychotic features cannot account for a manic episode meeting full duration and symptom criteria; a manic episode mandates a bipolar spectrum diagnosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder with mood instability emerging in the post-episode recovery and stabilisation phase
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder requires symptoms under one month with full return to premorbid function; recurrent presentations over two years with persistent psychosis exclude this. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder bridges schizophrenia and mood disorder spectra. DSM-5-TR criteria require a major mood episode concurrent with criterion A schizophrenia symptoms, at least 2 weeks of delusions or hallucinations in the absence of prominent mood symptoms across the lifetime of the illness, and that mood symptoms are present for the majority of the total duration. Bipolar subtype is used when manic episodes have occurred; depressive subtype when only depressive episodes have.
key_points: Schizoaffective needs at least 2 weeks of psychosis without prominent mood
Bipolar subtype requires a manic episode
Bipolar I with psychotic features confines psychosis to mood episodes
Schizophrenia requires mood episodes to be brief relative to overall illness
explanation.pearls: Key DSM-5 differentiator: at least 2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT prominent mood symptoms makes schizoaffective; psychosis only during mood episodes makes bipolar I with psychotic features.

sources.label: RANZCP Practice Guidelines: Schizophrenia and Related Disorders

stem: A 44-year-old man with bipolar I disorder has been stable on lithium 1000 mg nocte for 6 years with steady-state levels of 0.7 mmol/L. He attends for his routine review with you as his community psychiatry registrar. His baseline 6 years ago showed normal renal function with creatinine 78 micromol/L. He has no new symptoms, takes no nephrotoxic medications, drinks alcohol minimally, and adheres well to his lithium regimen. Today's bloods are reviewed alongside his historical trends.

data_table.Examination: No tremor; no oedema; mental state stable euthymic
data_table.Investigations: Lithium level 0.7 mmol/L; Cr 102 micromol/L (baseline 78); eGFR 68 mL/min/1.73m2 (baseline 92); TSH 3.2 mIU/L; calcium 2.42 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step regarding his renal monitoring?

options.text: Continue 6-monthly U+E and increase frequency only when the eGFR falls below the routine clinical action threshold
options.rationale: A persistent eGFR decline of around 25 percent from baseline over 6 years signals chronic lithium nephrotoxicity and warrants escalation rather than waiting for a worse eGFR. (Source: RANZCP Lithium Monitoring Guidance)

options.text: Repeat U+E and eGFR within 4 weeks, calculate the rate of decline, and arrange nephrology review for shared planning
options.rationale: A trajectory of progressive decline with eGFR drop of 25 percent over years signals lithium-induced chronic interstitial nephropathy; confirming the rate and involving nephrology for risk-benefit re-evaluation is the recommended pathway. (Source: RANZCP Lithium Monitoring Guidance)

options.text: Cease lithium today and start sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily as the immediate substitute mood stabiliser regimen
options.rationale: Abrupt lithium cessation increases relapse risk markedly; switching is a deliberate decision after nephrology input and consideration of alternatives and tapering. (Source: RANZCP Lithium Monitoring Guidance)

options.text: Add an angiotensin receptor blocker to reduce intraglomerular pressure and continue the existing lithium dose unchanged
options.rationale: Adding ARB risks lithium toxicity through reduced renal perfusion and sodium handling and does not arrest lithium nephropathy; nephrology assessment first guides safe management. (Source: RANZCP Lithium Monitoring Guidance)

options.text: Halve the lithium dose now to reduce nephrotoxic exposure and target a level of 0.4 mmol/L at the next steady state
options.rationale: Halving dose risks loss of mood control and subtherapeutic levels without nephrology input; arbitrary dose reduction is not the appropriate response to a declining eGFR trend. (Source: RANZCP Lithium Monitoring Guidance)

explanation.summary: Lithium chronic interstitial nephropathy presents as gradual eGFR decline over years. Routine monitoring is 3-monthly lithium and 6-monthly renal, thyroid, and calcium tests. Decline of around 25 percent from baseline or eGFR under 60 prompts confirmation of rate and nephrology referral to weigh continuation, dose reduction with closer level monitoring, or switching. Abrupt cessation risks relapse and is not done without a parallel plan.
key_points: Routine lithium monitoring: 3-monthly level, 6-monthly renal/thyroid/calcium
25 percent eGFR drop or eGFR under 60 triggers escalation
Confirm trajectory before changing therapy
Nephrology involvement guides continuation vs switch
explanation.pearls: Lithium nephropathy thresholds: confirm trajectory, refer nephrology when eGFR falls 25 percent from baseline or below 60 mL/min, do not stop lithium abruptly because relapse risk is high.
